data.title: SPLG: Just Another S&P 500 ETF, Except For One Important Thing
data.description:
Isn't SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:SPLG) just another index fund tracking that venerable index for investors in a single ticker? The answer depends essentially on one factor, or at most two. Let's explore.

As a background, since the 1950s, the Standard & Poor's 500 index has been maintained by a committee, which sets minimum standards for entry. For decades, the Dow Jones Industrial Average was widely considered the snapshot indicator for how the "stock market" was doing. Many investors still think of it that way.

However, the advent of indexed investing, first by Vanguard, and then, frankly, anyone else who could introduce profitable products around the S&P 500 index, vaulted the index to its iconic status. It is allocated across 11 economic sectors as shown below. That weighting is as of June 2025, but those will change over time as stock prices and valuations do. That's because the S&P 500 in its main form is "capitalization weighted." This simply means that the stock holdings in the index of about 500 companies impact the index as a whole based on how large the stocks are.

Much of the 21st century has been dominated by technology stocks. However, from a historical perspective, the US stock market has seen eras in which energy, industrials and other sectors have had a large impact on the S&P 500 index.

So, why is SPLG even needed? It debuted more than 12 years after State Street, which launched the first S&P 500 Index ETF and first US ETF of any type (SPY) back in 1993. Vanguard was already a pioneer in the field via its mutual fund presence, and it too entered the ETF business in 2010. And BlackRock's iShares unit introduced its own peer product way back in 2000.

The four ETFs shown above are charted below, over a 10-year period. In what is certainly an optical illusion, all four are actually pictured below. But their performance is so highly correlated, the naked eye cannot tell them apart.

As with other S&P 500 ETFs, perhaps the biggest risk of SPLG is the weighting structure. Markets are inherently cyclical. One prominent cycle throughout US stock market history is that big stocks get bigger, and that makes SPLG's capitalization-weighted setup that much more profitable.

However, in each prior era, at some point the cycle reversed, and smaller companies within the S&P 500 outperformed. This type of changing of the guard, so to speak, has caused SPLG to underperform its equal-weighted counterpart by as much as 100% over a 5-year time frame at one point this century.

Is that a future risk? Yes, even if it never happens again. Because as investors, risk is not what actually happens, it is what realistically could happen.

SPLG: digging deeper to know what you own in this ETF

While the answer to "what do I own if I buy SPLG" is nearly identical to if you owned one of the other leading S&P 500 tracking ETFs, in all cases, this is really about understanding what is in the S&P 500, its structure and what makes it tick. We review the sector weights and top 10 holdings above. Now let's continue with a closer look.

This information below is a snapshot in time, but since this article is not about a timely opinion on SPLG's investment attractiveness (or lack thereof), the focus here is on the left side of the table, the fundamentals, with some additional historical context explained below. SPLG has been around long enough that we can essentially view it as a learning tool about the nature of the S&P 500 itself. Let's dig in.

Dividend yield has at time been an attraction point for S&P 500 investors. However, the 21st century has mostly been about earnings growth and companies using their excess cash flow to redeploy, either back into the business or via buying back their own stock. That has made SPLG's dividend less of a "feature" than in the past. As we see below, long-term investors should recognize that in some of the worst market panics such as 2008, the yield on SPLG might spike to a level that is more attractive to buy into. Simply put, an SPLG dividend yield of about 2.5% or higher has historically led to strong long-term results.

Next, here's a backward-looking view of the S&P 500's price-earnings ratio. With perfect hindsight, since we know that the 15 years post-global financial crisis were a historically strong one for the index and thus for SPLG, this can help assess long-term valuation.

Specifically, a P/E Ratio below 15x is quite inexpensive. And north of 30x or even 25x, investors are assuming more than normal risk. 20x earnings has been about the 21st century norm, at least through the first quarter of the century.

Finally, let's examine price to sales. Many investors believe that sales is a better indicator of valuation than earnings, since earnings are easier to "manage" with accounting tricks. Here we see that in hindsight, SPLG below 1.6x sales was cheap, 2.0 less cheap and 3.0... well, we won't predict the future here! And only note that 3.0 is historically high.

What type of stocks are in the SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF?

Again, this is a snapshot at a point in time. However, SPLG is at all times a large cap stock index ETF. Even if the stock market collapses, it will still own the 500 largest stocks that meet the criteria set by the committee that runs the index. And SPLG will track that, move for move, stock by stock.

If a stock is technically considered smaller than midsized, it is likely because it has fallen in value, but is still in the index. And, over time, the industry has created a capitalization category called "Giant" to account for just how large some stocks became, while others are merely "Large" in size. The flip side of that is what we saw above, where a small number of stocks might at times dominate the index. Historically, that has worked out well for a while, but ultimately ended up poorly, with prices of the giants falling hard.

So, what is it about SPLG that makes it worth considering for some investors?

The answer is below. And while each investor needs to do their own research, if we go back to the original reason that the ETF sponsor (State Street) brought SPLG to market way back in 2005, the rationale makes sense. Specifically, it trades at a much lower dollar price than its peers. It always has, and barring stock splits, it always will.

This was very intentional. Because while big money investors do not think much about the dollar price of their ETF holdings, it does cost several hundred dollars to buy just one full share of SPY, IVV and VOO. But SPLG's price point allows investors to get access to the S&P 500 in ETF form, and spend a fraction of the cost of the others to get that one share. Thus, SPLG's main advantage is for smaller investors.

The expense ratio is slightly lower than the peers. That likely accounts for part of the attraction of SPLG for investors of all sizes. For devout S&P 500 index investors, every bit of cost counts, since the net return is bound to be slightly higher over time. Note that such an advantage is too small to show up in chart included above.

Implications for options investing

SPLG at $70 a share has another advantage over the rest. While its options market is less liquid than SPY's, it is liquid enough for many investors to use SPLG instead of SPY when applying options strategies. The "ante" to buy 100 shares, what is needed to hedge with put options or write covered call options against (each option contract represents 100 shares of SPLG) is the key here. For a $70 ETF, that's $7,000. For a $600 ETF, that's $60,000. So, the lower dollar price, which often does not matter to investors, might matter a lot here.

For instance, if an investor wanted to write out of the money puts. With the SPLG at $70, the July 66 strike put (6% OTM) is priced at $0.50, and the margin required is just under $1000. Conversely, with the SPY trading at $597, the July $560 (6% OTM) strike put is priced at $4.36, and the margin requirement is just over $8200. There is also the covered call aspect. To own 100 shares of SPY takes $59,700 in order to have the 100 shares to write a covered call on your position. Whereas owning 100 shares of SPLG only requires $7000.

So, SPLG has a purpose, one that has given its rightful place alongside the kingpins of S&P 500 index investing, including its cousin ETF, the mighty SPY. Does that mean any particular investor should choose SPLG for S&P 500 exposure? That's up to everyone to decide for themselves. But at least they can do so with full information, given the transparency and freedom of choice that characterize modern investment markets.

This article answers these three main questions about SPLG:

  1. What is the one big difference between SPLG and SPY?
  2. Does SPLG have an appeal to smaller retail investors?
  3. Is SPLG's expense ratio lower than most ETFs?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Block: Darkest Before Dawn For My Top Pick
data.description:
Block (NYSE:XYZ) has struggled as the company met deteriorating confidence in its business model with a reduction in full-year guidance. Investor confidence may be improving as of late after a subsequent investor conference which appeared to imply positive trends in the second quarter, but it remains true that the company is leaning into increasing its loan businesses even in the face of an uncertain macro picture. While one could quibble on the quality of the growth rates, the company still maintains a net cash balance sheet, is GAAP profitable, and is aggressively repurchasing stock. The valuation looks too conservative, and I expect margin expansion to be a key catalyst for a re-rating in the quarters ahead. XYZ remains a top pick.

XYZ Stock Price

I last covered XYZ in April, where I named the stock my top overall pick after the stock’s brutal slide. The company proceeded to deliver disappointing results, causing the stock to sink even further before recovering its losses and posting a 15% gain.

It is always darkest before dawn, and some red flags are emerging - but I remain partial to the positive fundamental characteristics and low valuation.

XYZ Stock Key Metrics

XYZ is a collection of three apparently unrelated businesses, with those being the famous Square point of sale products, financial Cash app, and the new Bitcoin mining operations. The company’s digital-first footprint makes it a financial tech company (‘fintech’) but its diversification makes it more unique relative to other more-pure-play peers.

The latest quarter saw the company deliver 9% YoY gross profit growth to $2.29 billion, missing guidance of $2.32 billion. This miss was particularly concerning given that the guidance had previously been viewed as already disappointing, with the stock also falling considerably following fourth quarter results. XYZ notably delivered among the weakest results as compared to other fintech names in my coverage.

Profitability remains the company’s lone bright spot, with the company posting another GAAP profitable quarter.

The company delivered $466 million of adjusted operating income, representing a 20% margin and notably beating guidance of $430 million. What’s more, the company repurchased $445 million of stock, the most aggressive that I have seen from the company. These two factors could have saved the stock, but I suspect that investors need to see more quarters before allowing for bottom-line strength to offset top-line weakness.

Cash App, which has traditionally been the primary driver of growth, saw gross profit growth plummet to just 10% in the quarter.

This was caused in part due to the company seeing user growth stagnate for the 5th straight quarter. I had expected user growth to return after 4 prior quarters of stagnation.

Square gross profit grew 9% YoY which was a solid result given that the company was lapping tough comparables.

The company saw the greatest growth overseas, with international GPV growing 15% YoY.

The company ended the quarter with $7.1 billion of cash versus $5.1 billion of debt, representing a strong net cash balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management has reduced full-year expectations to see just 12% YoY gross profit growth, significantly down from the prior 15% forecast. Management also reduced the adjusted operating margin guidance to 19%. I note that I find the margin target to look quite conservative, given that the company just posted a 20% number.

For the second quarter, management has guided the company to see 9.5% YoY gross profit growth.

On the conference call, management noted that these new targets reflect a greater degree of conservatism than in the past - but investors can be forgiven for retaining some skepticism given this latest guidance miss.

In spite of ongoing tariff uncertainty, Management undertook a somewhat risky move in offering preliminary expectations for the third and fourth quarter, to see around 12% and 15% gross profit growth, respectively. Management noted that in March, the company received FDIC approval to use Square Financial Services to originate Cash App Borrow loans. This would double the number of users that can be offered Cash App loans as well as improving the unit economics of these loans. Management also was upbeat about integrating Afterpay into Cash app and the associated uplift in BNPL originations. I note that both of these catalysts have the unfortunate implication of a move lower in business model quality, as investors likely prefer the traditional fintech model over the banking model. Management did note expectations to deliver their first bitcoin mining chips in the second half of the year, which might offer some offset to the above issue.

Many investors left that call with a pessimistic view of the stock, as it seemed like the company was seeing a steep slowdown in its underlying businesses. However, at a subsequent investor conference, management noted that they had seen a clear improvement in the second quarter, with Cash App gross profit coming in at 13% YoY for the month of April, up from 7% in the month of March. Management also noted that inflows per active improved to 9% growth YoY in April versus just 5% in March. It is interesting that management only decided to share these tidbits at this subsequent conference versus at its earnings call, which occurred on May 1st, but I digress.

XYZ also hosted its first-ever biannual product release, where it unveiled, among other things, an impressive handheld POS system.

XYZ has had a reputation for lagging peers in terms of innovation - this inaugural release event struck a different tone that appeared to signal a concerted effort to reignite the growth engine.

Is XYZ Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

XYZ continues to trade as one of the cheapest tech stocks in my coverage universe. The stock recently traded hands at just around 22x earnings, with earnings expected to grow rapidly over the coming years. I note that 2029 earnings estimates imply a 40% net margin based on gross profits, which looks quite reasonable in my opinion. The stock trades at just 6.7x 2029 earning estimates.

Consensus estimates call for the company to generate around double-digit top-line growth over the coming years.

At first glance, the stock might not look that cheap, especially given that it appears to be making efforts to become more and more like a bank given management guidance to increase its lending program. However, we mustn’t forget that XYZ is most certainly not a bank, in that it does not carry the same balance sheet risk. XYZ has a net cash balance sheet in stark contrast with traditional banks which typically leverage customer deposits. Moreover, the company’s Square and Protomining businesses appear to still be growing healthily and arguably represent high-quality businesses.

The stock recently traded hands at just around 4.2x gross profits. Based on my projection for 40% net margins over the long term, that represents a 10.5x long-term earnings multiple. The company’s net cash balance sheet and aggressive share repurchase program make that valuation look too low, as I’d be at a loss to identify other stocks that stayed at such conservative multiples while carrying such characteristics. I expect the stock to re-rate to a 6x sales multiple at the very least, implying a stock price of around $90 per share and representing a 15x long-term earnings multiple. That appears to be a fair valuation given the company’s resilient top-line growth, strong balance sheet, and improving profitability, while still offering even more upside should the company execute strongly on accelerating growth rates.

XYZ Stock Risks

XYZ appears to be facing a perfect storm between competition and macro headwinds, as well as an intentional push to increase the lending business, which might pressure the valuation multiple. The strong balance sheet and GAAP profitability suggest that the valuation has already priced in such risks, and share repurchases may help take advantage if low valuations persist. That said, it is possible that the projected acceleration in top-line growth in the second half might not prove to be an adequate catalyst if the market associates the increasing lending business as being more cyclical and deserving of lower multiples. Investors hoping for a re-rating may need to wait for XYZ to maximize profit margins, which might be a harder catalyst relative to typical growth levers.

XYZ Stock Conclusion

XYZ might not be offering as exciting growth as other peers, but the stock looks too cheap here. The company maintains a net cash balance sheet, has rapidly expanding GAAP profit margins, and is aggressively repurchasing stock. I view it to be just a matter of time before the share repurchase program highlights the cheap valuation, while the inaugural biannual product release event might even be signaling an inflection in growth rates. XYZ is a core holding of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio and remains one of my top picks.

data.title: Kinsale Capital: It's Great, But Overpricing Risks And Bearish Signals Are Coming (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Three months after my previous coverage, Kinsale Capital Group (NYSE: KNSL) remains a solid P&C insurance company despite uncertainty surrounding the property market. Its disciplined underwriting, effective cost management, and strong Balance Sheet remain its cornerstones. Yet, I have noticed some recent changes, which we should also take into consideration. Its upside seems limited after its price increased by $50 from my first article. Technical risks appear as momentum wavers.

Positives

As we all know, Kinsale Capital Group, Inc. is a well-diversified business operating across various insurance niches. Primarily, it offers P&C insurance. Yet, it also offers other insurance types across all 50 types. Even in locations, KNSL is strategic as it has a high concentration in cities with many high-net-worth individuals or HNWIs. I already explained this before. This is one of the factors I like about it. This gives it underwriting and pricing flexibility and security despite its exposure to hurricane-prone states. This also allows the company to maintain its huge domestic market dominance and popularity, especially in the E&S insurance market. Its competitive advantage in this niche allows it to write about $155M in E&S premiums per year or about $40M per quarter. Considering all this, it’s not surprising to see its gross written premiums in all insurance types increase by 8.0% YoY from $448.6M to $484.3M.

I also like the fact that the company remains conservative and strategic amid its sustained growth. Its ceded premiums cover at least 20% of the gross premiums. This allows KNSL to diversify or pass on risks to reinsurers. That way, it will limit the amount it might bleed out in case of unfortunate events to its policyholders like hurricanes, fire, and accidents. Currently, it has $374M in reinsurance recoverable, which can cover 15% of unpaid claims in liabilities.

Lastly, I like its strong Balance Sheet, which promises long-term sustainability. As an insurance company, it’s not surprising to see it hold a large amount of liquid assets, such as cash and investments. Their combined amount can cover all liabilities at once even if it doesn’t have to. In addition, its investments are well-balanced, although geared more toward risk aversion. Most of these are fixed-maturity securities, allowing it to stabilize investment yields amid market volatility. As a result, its investment income keeps increasing.

Concerns

When I previously covered KNSL, there were concerns that either I did not notice or had not emerged yet. Now, I am listing them one by one to balance my view of this company.

One thing I noticed after the release of Q1 results was that growth has become toppish. Before Q1 2024, its total revenue growth was at least 6%. But since Q2, growth has started to wane. In Q2, quarterly revenue growth was only 3.1%. This was already half the previous growth rate. In Q4, its QoQ revenue change weakened some more to -1.4%. This Q1 2025, it rebounded a bit but remained weak at 2.6%. We may attribute it to macroeconomic headwinds as inflation remains stubborn. Policy rate changes also affected the yields of debt securities.

Yet, what I really think is that the P&C insurance demand growth is starting to become saturated. It may be due to the fact that new demand or the number of new policyholders is decreasing. This is mostly applicable in the P&C insurance niche. Note that existing home sales, inventory, and construction starts have dropped in recent years. In addition, vacancies are increasing as median home prices in the US remain high. The thing is, there is no housing shortage in general. The real shortage applies to affordable housing. With the current demand and supply mechanism, I believe the price will stay at its current range for an extended period. After all, property builders have learned from the Great Recession by building fewer and slower to manage prices. With fewer new homes and homeowners, the inflow of new P&C insurance policyholders may decrease. In turn, its revenue growth may continue to soften.

I’m not saying that we should avoid this stock. No, that’s not my point. I just think that it will take a lot of time for its revenue growth to accelerate. I also think it may remain somewhat flattish. But with its solid customer base in the E&S segment and its robust underwriting discipline, KNSL will be fine.

My other concern is that its moat may be emulated in the long run. To be honest, I really like its enhanced underwriting and claims processes. Thanks to its heavy investment in technology, especially data analytics and machine learning in recent years. This move helped increase risk assessment accuracy, improve pricing accuracy, reduce underwriting time, and prevent data breaches. All this contributed to its low expense ratio of only 20.0% versus 20.7% YoY. However, the continued digital revolution and the emergence of AI will make it possible for other insurance companies to copy this model. It may take some years, of course. But it will be better if KNSL prepares for it. On a lighter note, the company has already fully grasped this approach. It has already perfected its technology utilization, so it still has a huge edge over its competitors.

Valuation

KNSL has already increased by over $40 or 11% since my previous coverage. At this point, P/E still suggests a strong buy rating as the stock trades at 27x versus the five-year average of 31x. This suggests a TP of $539.73 or another 14% upside.

However, other key metrics like book value per share, sales per share, and cash flow per share advise us to be more careful now. P/B shows that the stock trades at 6.98x its BVPS, which is already higher than the 6.82x average. If we use this to derive its fair value, the TP will be $463.22 or a 2.2% downside risk. The same can be observed in P/S and P/FCF.

On a lighter note, one thing is common among the three ratios. That is the downside risk remains low, below 4%. Somehow, we can tell that valuation risks and my concerns have already been priced in. Nonetheless, we should still be cautious since they imply limited upside.

Technicals

KNSL has stayed resilient amid the mass stock price correction recently. However, I advise investors to take some time to strategize before rushing into it. We are seeing early bearish signals now. The price just crossed below both the 50-day SMA and the 200-day SMA. Meanwhile. The 50-day SMA has started to decrease, warning us of a potential shift in market sentiments. Although it has not officially crossed below the 200-day SMA, the probability is high. Once it happens, this will be a Death Cross Formation, which can imply a bearish reversal or at least a pullback. I cannot verify it yet as it continues to create higher lows, so support is not broken yet. A possible confirmation is the fact that the price consolidates at $465-478.

Its MACD and RSI also advise caution. I have already noticed a bearish divergence last April 24. That was when the price created a new high, but momentum moved in contrast. That could indicate decreasing buying pressures and/or profit-taking. In addition, MACD is moving slightly below the signal line and the histogram is already in the negative region. The consolation is that the RSI is still below the overbought region, so a buy position is still possible but limited. Coincidentally, we can note the price reaction to the Fibonacci Retracement. Whenever the body and/or the wick of the candlestick touches any point, the price direction shifts immediately. Considering this, it is essential that the stock price touches or breaks above the 0.786 retracement point. This is within the resistance zone. If not, there is a high probability that the price will reverse or dip.

Conclusion

Kinsale Capital Group is a strong company with a well-diversified business model and a well-positioned Balance Sheet. Its robust underwriting discipline, solid customer base, and technological investment ensure profitability and sustainability. However, its slowing growth coupled with potential overpricing can limit its upside. Technical risks are present amid early bearish signals. I’m downgrading my rating on this stock from strong buy to hold.

data.title: Whitehaven Coal: A Growing Coal Miner With A Potential Trading Opportunity Around The Corner
data.description:
Whitehaven Coal: an Aussie miner expanding into metallurgical coal

Whitehaven Coal (US over-the-counter ticker: OTCPK:WHITF; ASX ticker: WHC) is a coal mining firm founded 25 years ago in Sydney, Australia. The company has been for most of its life focused on top-quality thermal coal production, but recently expended into metallurgical coal production.

Thermal coal, or steam coal, is the type of coal used to generate heat and is mostly consumed by power plants. By contrast, metallurgical coal is used in the scope of steel production. There are several ways to produce steel, but the most common one consists in transforming hard coking coal into metallurgical coke and then using this coke to turn iron ore and limestone into crude iron. This is why many people use the term hard coking coal, or simply coking coal, when referring to metallurgical coal, but these words are actually not similar because hard coking coal only refers to one specific type of metallurgical coal. The two other types of metallurgical coal are semi-soft coking coal, usually simply called semi-soft, and PCI coal. As shown by Figure 1, there is a continuum between coal grades so that PCI coal and semi-soft coal, while formally categorized as metallurgical coal grades, can also theoretically be consumed by power producers as long as it makes economic sense for them to do so.

A growing number of countries across the globe are actively trying to reduce carbon emissions in the power sector by switching from coal to gas or replacing coal plants with greener alternatives like renewables or nuclear energy. On top of this, the world's two largest consumers of thermal coal, India and China, have been boosting domestic supply to cut reliance on seaborne foreign coal. As a result, the long-term outlook for thermal coal exporters is rather bearish. Regarding metallurgical coal, the long-term picture remains relatively bullish, as greener coal-free alternatives remain expensive. As a result, most of the steel mills being constructed continue to be coal-based, as illustrated by India (Figure 2).

For Whitehaven Coal, fiscal year 2023, or FY23, was the last exercise during which more than 80% of its production and sales were made of thermal coal. During this 12-month period, the company sold a total of 16.62 million metric tons of coal, with the top four destinations, Japan, Taiwan, South Korea and Malaysia, accounting for no less than 94% of all the cargoes exported! At that time, all these four countries had already pledged to become carbon-neutral by 2050. It was consequently crystal clear to the management team that Whitehaven Coal needed not only to look for new markets for its thermal coal cargoes but also to diversify.

Such an opportunity to diversify occurred at the beginning of FY24 and Whitehaven Coal did not miss it. Hence, in October 2023, the company agreed to purchase two metallurgical coal mines located in Queensland from fellow miner BHP Mitsubishi Alliance: Blackwater and Daunia. It is worth reminding here that the purchase was opposed by some of Whitehaven's large shareholders who wanted the company's large pile of cash to fund a special dividend and not what they described as a risky bet but the management team held the line and a majority of shareholders eventually backed this investment.

For those unfamiliar with Australia, most coal cargoes are basically exported from the East Coast. Thermal coal cargoes are generally produced in New South Wales (NSW) and exported via the port of Newcastle, while metallurgical coal usually comes from the Bowen Basin in Queensland (QLD) and is exported via Hay Point or Gladstone. The little map below should help you visualize!

The impact of Daunia and Blackwater on Whitehaven's bottom line was immediate

Whitehaven Coal officially took control of these two metallurgical coal mines in the last quarter of fiscal year 2024 (i.e., April-June 2024, as the Australian fiscal year runs from July to June). Due to the fact that both Daunia and Blackwater were mature assets, the impact on Whitehaven's production profile and revenue was instantaneous, as well as massive.

Data presented by Figure 4 and Figure 5 show that, in FY24, while metallurgical coal only accounted for 16.4% of the total sales, in volume, its share in total revenue was closer to 30%. Also, in the first nine months of FY25, metallurgical coal accounted for about 50% of the company's sales and 60% of the total revenue.

In other words, the company has doubled its production capacity with this acquisition and its total revenue even more! Due to the fact that Whitehaven partially funded the purchase with its massive cash reserves, its total debt, just below A$1 billion, remains relatively limited and should be easily manageable for a company of this size.

Was the purchase of Daunia and Blackwater a good deal for Whitehaven?

Whitehaven Coal purchased the two metallurgical coal mines for US$3.2 billion in cash. On top of this sum, US$900 million in additional payments will be due if average sales price remains above US$159 per metric ton over the next several years. Whitehaven Coal subsequently sold 30% of the Blackwater mine to Japanese consumers Nippon Steel (20%) and JFE Steel (10%) for US$ 1.08 billion in summer 2024. As a result, Whitehaven actually paid US$ 1.92 billion for a full ownership of Daunia and a 67% stake in Blackwater. As shown by Figure 6, these assets do not produce the same type of coal products and have a vastly different life expectancy profile.

Overall, this looks like a good deal for Whitehaven as well. Even if we assume that the company will need to pay the additional US$900 million over the coming years, Whitehaven paid a total of US$3.02 billion to increase its coal reserves by 196 million metric tons, with two thirds of this addition being good-quality hard coking coal. This broadly translates into a cost of US$15 per metric ton of un-mined coal and even in the current weak pricing environment, Whitehaven is still able to secure a larger margin. Indeed, in the first six months of FY25 (i.e., July 2024-December 2024), the reported average mining cost was A$137 per metric ton with royalties, A$27 per metric ton, coming on top of it. This translates into a total cost of A$164 per metric ton. Meanwhile, the average sales price for metallurgical coal cargo was reported at A$247 per metric ton over the period. In other words, the average margin was A$83 per metric ton, or about US$54 per metric ton, way above the US$15 per metric ton paid for these assets!

Having said that, actual data suggest that a lot of work still needs to be done for the targets presented by Figure 7 to become reality. Indeed, neither Daunia nor Blackwater is expected to meet its saleable coal production target in FY25 and the average production cost across the two mines was A$137 per metric ton in the first half of FY25, excluding royalties, way above the A$119-122 target. The team of executives leading Whitehaven, on top of which comes CEO Paul Flynn, who has been at the helm for more than 13 years, is aware of these challenges and has already presented a series of solutions. For Daunia, for example, the company plans to reduce mining cost through three major methods: by combining some of the tasks with Whitehaven's close-by Wincester South project to achieve significant economies of scale; by adopting a fully autonomous trucking fleet and upgrading the processing plant used to wash raw coal; and by reducing the mine's strip ratio through dragline optimization.

Is thermal coal to be abandoned?

As of today, the company has absolutely no plan to abandon thermal coal, despite its wish to slowly increase its focus on metallurgical coal. Two reasons explain this choice. First, thermal coal mining is currently as profitable for the company as metallurgical coal production (Figure 7). Second, Whitehaven produces some of the best-quality thermal coal cargoes in Australia and even around the globe.

Most coal producers sell their cargoes at a discount to the Newcastle price index, as it is difficult for thermal coal cargoes to meet all the required specifications. Adjustments related to sulfur, ash or moisture content are really common, on top of those related to calorific value. In the case of Whitehaven, the average sales price for its thermal coal cargoes was 108% of the Newcastle index in the most recent quarter. A truly impressive number! Hence, as long as there is demand for thermal coal in Asia, there will likely be demand for Whitehaven's products.

The fact that Whitehaven is still developing thermal coal projects and expending current thermal coal operations (Figure 8) clearly demonstrates that the company has no plan to abandon thermal coal in the short term.

EPS highlights continuously profitable operations

As shown by Figure 9, since FY15, the company only reported negative annual earnings per share twice, and it is important to note that, in both cases, this negative value was due to impairment losses and not to operations turning cash-negative. In both cases, the recorded paper loss was superior to the total net loss, suggesting that Whitehaven has actually been able to keep its mining operations profitable over the entire 10-year period. This is not a small performance coming from a coal mining firm or any other single commodity producer.

Has the time come to accumulate?

P/E ratio: Whitehaven against peers

Let's compare Whitehaven's price-to-earnings ratio with its ASX-listed peers, namely Yancoal, New Hope Group, Stanmore Resources and Coronado Global Resources. For those unfamiliar with these names, Figure 10 should give you an idea about their respective market capitalization. For comparison, Peabody Energy's market capitalization is currently around US$1.6 billion or A$2.4 billion.

Looking at P/E ratio when dealing with coal miners, and more generally with single commodity producers, may be confusing, as numbers are often all over the place and can be quite misleading. In theory, the lower the P/E ratio is, the more attractive the share becomes but if we look at Figure 11, it appears that for most miners, the lowest P/E was achieved in either FY22 or FY23. At that time, P/E ratio was low because coal spot prices were abnormally high, thanks to the conflict in Ukraine. If you invested in any of these miners at that time, your investment would be significantly underwater by now (by potentially as much as 50% in the case of Whitehaven).

Even worse, in FY23, Whitehaven's coal P/E ratio was not only recorded at a record low of 2.22, if we ignore negative values, but the company also displayed the lowest P/E ratio among peers. In other words, it was theoretically the perfect time to accumulate WHITF shares. Since then, the share price has fallen by more than 15%!

Because commodities are highly cyclical and P/E ratio tends to crater when commodity prices spike, it may actually be dangerous to rely on P/E ratio when dealing with single commodity producers like Whitehaven, as you may actually end up being caught in a bull trap.

This is why, for a single commodity producer, I find the P/E ratio, taken at face value or compared to peers, inconclusive, and prefer using the price-to-book ratio, as rigid accounting rules used to calculate assets and liabilities tend to flatten out the volatility of commodity prices.

P/B ratio: a more reliable indicator for single commodity producers

Figure 12 below shows that the price-to-book ratio for Australian coal miners is less volatile than the price-to-earnings ratio.

Since FY15, Whitehaven has only displayed once the lowest P/B ratio among its peers. This happened in FY20, when the Covid pandemic distorted companies' valuations for months. This may probably never happen under normal conditions, as Whitehaven shares, massively owned by institutional investors, generally command a slight premium compared to peers. As a result, the right way to apprehend Whitehaven's P/B ratio seems to be by comparing the current face value to past occurrences (i.e., is the current P/B ratio relatively high or low compared to previous years?) and also analyzing its relation to share price (i.e., at current P/B ratio, how did share price behave in the past?). Applied to Whitehaven, this method seems to be particularly successful.

Indeed, analysis shows that, since FY15, each time this P/B ratio was recorded below 1 on the last day of any fiscal year, accumulating shares on this specific day and holding them for 12 or 24 months, has proved to be a highly profitable trading strategy, as shown by Figure 13. Such a buying opportunity occurred five times over the past 10 years and may be about to present itself once again in about two weeks, as the current price-to-book ratio for Whitehaven Coal is below 1 (0.91) and the last day of FY25 around the corner (June 30). For those of you looking for simple buy-and-hold strategies, this may be worth a shot!

Internal buying activity

Whitehaven Coal executives have accumulated more than 52,000 shares since the beginning of the year (Figure 14). It may be argued that these purchases neither account for a significant portion of the tradable shares (more than 836 million) nor translate into a significant financial bet for these well-paid executives, as less than US$200,000 were spent in total. These arguments are valid but the absence of registered sales and the fact that all the purchased shares were acquired at market price suggests to me that the management team still sees significant upside for the company's share price.

About dividend and shares buyback

The company generally pays an interim dividend around February/March and a final and larger dividend following the closure of the fiscal year in June. A 2025 interim dividend of 9 Australian cents was paid in March 2025. If we conservatively assume another 9 Australian cents payment following the closure of FY25, this translates into a yield of 3.1% at the current share price. Not bad!

Due to the fact that Whitehaven is focused on keeping its mining operations profitable, the company is generally in a position to pay a dividend. This is one of the reasons why institutional funds like Whitehaven Coal. You will still notice by looking at Figure 15 that no dividend was paid in FY21. That year, a large impairment loss pushed earnings into negative territory, despite cash-generating mining activities, and the management then decided to skip dividend payment. Whitehaven Coal only pays the dividends it can afford!

When it comes to shares buyback, it is worth noting that in FY22 and FY23, the company purchased back about 20% of its shares for a total of A$1.3 billion. In February 2025, the company announced that it would resume this activity with a dedicated budget of "up to A$72 million" to be spent "over the next six months." At the current price, this represents about 13 million shares, or about 1.5% of the float. This is not enormous but can be easily achieved and always sends a positive signal to investors and shareholders.

What could go wrong?

A lot of things could push Whitehaven Coal's share price lower, on top of which comes a large drop in commodity prices. In a recent article on AMR, I explained why the outlook for metallurgical coal may not be as bullish as often presented. For thermal coal, the picture is not exactly bullish either, as China, the world's largest importer, is expected to cut foreign coal imports by up to 20% in 2025 to protect domestic supply amid falling demand. The Newcastle Coal Index for thermal coal has been stabilizing around US$100-110 per metric ton in recent weeks but support may simply be the result of the heavy rains which negatively impacted coal deliveries across New South Wales, leading to major queues at Newcastle port.

Beyond coal pricing, weather events, carbon policies and the A$/US$ exchange rate could also negatively impact Whitehaven Coal:

1/ Flooding and wildfires are two major risks for coal miners in Australia. These events are by definition unpredictable, but can easily and significantly disturb coal mining and/or railing activities. These events generally force miners to declare force majeure and translate into lower coal sales.

2/ The Labor government in Australia, recently reelected, is committed to introducing tougher GHG emissions regulations. Whitehaven Coal emits a lot of carbon dioxide each year. In FY24, for instance, the company released more than 1.3 million metric tons of CO2-equivalent. 55% of this total was methane from fugitive emissions. According to Ember, Whitehaven's methane emissions could explode over the next 25 years. It is unclear at this stage how the company may be required, through incoming policies, to offset these emissions, but it is important to keep in mind that this may potentially have a massive impact on Whitehaven finances. This is a major risk but not a short-term one. If you plan to hold your shares for a year or two, you will probably find this risk to be negligible.

3/ The A$/US$ exchange rate also represents a potential risk for the company's earnings. At US$ 0.65 per Australian dollar, the US currency is historically strong (Figure 16). For Whitehaven Coal, whose expenses are in Australian dollars and sales in US dollars, a strong US dollar represents a clear tailwind. Any strengthening in the value of the Aussie dollar would directly and negatively impact Whitehaven Coal's earnings. The impact could be exacerbated in the context of low sales prices.

Conclusion

I like Whitehaven Coal and have been a happy shareholder for years. The company offers stable cash-generating mining activities and has a clearly defined growth strategy. Contrary to most coal miners, the company has decided to stick to thermal coal and develop metallurgical coal mining activities in parallel. The current valuation, based on price-to-book ratio and its relation with share price over the past 10 years, seems attractive. The management team, focused and competent, continues to prioritize cash flow while remaining shareholder-friendly.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Micron Q3 Preview: I Predicted This Surge, And It's Not Too Late
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Micron (NASDAQ:MU), the stock has gained 19% in price.

As investors gear up for Micron's Q3 FY2025 earnings, it’s important to look through the surface optimism to understand the underlying strategic developments taking place. The cycle emerging from ever-increasing memory consumption linked to AI is structurally different from the past. Understanding Micron's distinct positioning in the memory market as the leader in High Bandwidth Memory ("HBM") with massive U.S. capital investment is critical to understanding the stock’s potential. Investors should be acutely focused on management's subtle signals regarding regionally stabilized pricing outlook and inventory build. The upside potential is significant, but cyclical and geopolitical realities will decide whether Micron is a medium-term holding or better as a short-term cyclical play at this time.

Q3 Is Set To Be A Blockbuster Moment

Micron is guiding for a record-setting Q3 FY2025 with projected revenue of $8.8 billion ± $0.2 billion and non-GAAP EPS (earnings per share) of $1.57 ± $0.10. This is a sequential increase over Q2's $8.05 billion, reflecting healthy demand from both AI and the data center market. Management forecasts gross margins at approximately 36.5%, a decline from Q1's ~39% number, due to NAND pricing and product mix.

The NAND market is especially difficult, with an overall industry supply/demand imbalance continuing to negatively impact the market price for memory. Micron is actively addressing challenges with NAND given that it is underutilizing its NAND manufacturing facilities and has announced plans to reduce wafer capacity by over 10% year-over-year through FY2025. Though intended to stabilize pricing, these plans take immediate margin impact. Overall, storage revenue was down 20% in Q2, while mobile revenue was down 30%, as OEMs (original equipment manufacturers) work their way through the excess inventory.

However, in spite of market headwinds, Micron continues to emphasize its strategic investment plan. Capital expenditure ("capex") will continue to be substantial; the guidance is over $3 billion for Q3, and Micron has pointed to overall capex guidance of ~$14 billion for FY2025. This encompasses significant initiatives like a new DRAM manufacturing facility in Idaho, an HBM production facility in Singapore, and a testing center in Taiwan. While none of these will affect output in the short term, they set up Micron for longer-term growth opportunities.

Operating cash flow remains healthy, at $3.9 billion in Q2, and the company has $9.6 billion in cash and marketable securities. Management has reiterated its capital expenditure plans for the coming months, and investors will be looking for more guidance from management during Q3 earnings discussions.

Strategic Catalysts Fuel The Engine For Returns

Micron is positioning itself in the memory space in support of AI, and HBM is at the core of its strategic vision. In Fiscal Q2, HBM revenue crossed $1 billion for the quarter, up over 50% from Q1, and this growth stemmed mainly from demand for AI accelerators, like Nvidia's (NVDA) H100, HGX, GH200/GB200, and AMD's (AMD) MI350. Micron's HBM3E is already designed into both Nvidia's GH200/GB200 and GB300 platforms, and AMD's MI350. Micron's HBM supply is sold out through the end of 2025 and new deals for 2026 are in progress, and management expects the total HBM market to be greater than $35 billion next year. By Q4 FY2025, management expects HBM to reflect a DRAM share consistent with Micron's overall DRAM footprint.

Micron is also advancing the roadmap for HBM4, which is due in 2026. Based on Micron's estimates, HBM4 will offer more than 60% more bandwidth than HBM3E, and given the silicon intensity of HBM (3–4 times the wafer usage relative to DDR5), supply will remain structurally tight, supporting pricing power. In addition to HBM, Micron is also pushing its DRAM roadmap with LPDDR5X, targeted at smartphones and edge AI. The 1β node is already being mass-produced, and Micron is collaborating with Nvidia on a new artificial intelligence-optimized memory form factor (SO-CAMM).

To support long-term leadership, Micron plans a $200 billion expansion in the U.S. over the next decade: $150 billion for manufacturing facilities and $50 billion for R&D, with $6.4 billion in CHIPS Act support secured. Two DRAM fabs are planned in Idaho, with up to four in New York, and domestic HBM packaging expansion in Virginia. This means shifting 40% of DRAM production to the U.S., which will increase supply chain resilience and align with U.S. policy.

Micron Is An Exemplary Portfolio Play Still In Action

When considering Micron's growth horizon (i.e. triple-digit FY25 normalized EPS growth and high double-digit FY26 growth), the stock price is valid at the moment, even with it trading above the 50-week moving average. This is no longer an undervalued stock, but it's reasonably valued and still has a moderate growth runway ahead of it. I expect that by the end of H1 2026, my price objective of about $145 will have been hit and I will be recycling the profits and initial invested capital into less cyclical and more durable growth investments. I bought Micron at an average price of $74 a share. My investment is now up by over 55%. Once I sell my position at around $145, my total return over about 12-18 months will be nearly 100%. Micron is one of my largest portfolio positions, at about 12% of my holdings. It is an exemplary selection that shows my strategy of sentiment analysis and capitalizing on cyclical dynamics for elite returns.

Cyclical Dynamics Are Registered By The Wise

Micron is in a stronger up-cycle following one of its worst down-cycles in 2023 when revenues were cut by almost half. For Q2 FY2025, revenues are about 38% higher year-over-year, with management forecasting record top-line revenue. DRAM prices, which are core to about 75% of Micron's revenue, have turned positive. NAND pricing remains weak but due to peers cutting production, has likely bottomed. The normalization of inventory, particularly in mobile and auto, affirms the argument for continued demand recovery.

Micron is trading at approximately 16.5x forward non-GAAP earnings, with FY2025 EPS of about $7 likely, meaning an increase of more than 400% from FY2024. The main discussion revolves around whether AI-led demand can extend the cycle and sustain margins. In the bull case, you could assert that AI, auto-part, and HBM leadership represent structural growth that supports an entirely new, less volatile paradigm. In the bear or base case, you could assert that cycles have always ended in oversupply, and large capacity growth could extend the cycle, but not indefinitely. I'm moderately bullish but defensive, keeping my $145 take-profit target engaged for now.

Macro Catalysts Can Impact The Cyclical Thesis

Micron's outlook is very promising; however, several risks exist. The company's $14 billion capex budget, in addition to its $200 billion expansion budget in the U.S., presents some execution risk. Any execution delays, cost overruns or a lower-than-anticipated ROI (return on investment) would negatively impact Micron's balance sheet, especially if government incentives fail to offset decreased profitability.

Geopolitical risks also loom larger as it relates to China. China has already banned certain Micron products, and any retaliation on their part could negatively impact sales, while export controls or tariffs could negatively impact Micron's supply chain or costs. I am hopeful of more peaceful U.S.-China relations moving forward, but this is dependent on state officials working diligently with state-of-the-art diplomacy and exemplary statesmanship, including emotional strategy.

Cyclicality remains a consistent risk. As previously noted, Micron is intelligently managing supply by appropriately cutting NAND supply and lowering inventory, and there is a risk of being oversupplied if we see a knee-jerk reaction from competitors or if stagnated demand (e.g., smartphones or data centers) does not meet expectations. NAND in particular could be negatively impacted.

Lastly, investor sentiment revolves greatly around management tone, and any bearish sentiment around guidance for Q4, flat gross margin outlook, or vague commentary on 2026 could indicate the peak of the cycle is closer than we’d hope. While Micron's performance in HBM and the new generation DDR5 has been very good, even a small mistake today may provide competitors the opportunity to catch up.

Keep An Eye On The Upcoming Earnings Call

The earnings call on June 25 will provide the most insight into what phase of the memory up-cycle we're in. The key earnings call questions will be: Is DRAM pricing still firm, and is NAND pricing leveling off or bottoming? If management mentions anything similar to "tight supply" or "pricing has firmed," it will support the bull case.

I’ll monitor margins closely. Q3 guidance was for 36–37% gross margins, but better than expected margin expansion depends on the level of HBM products sold and the progress of cost-per-bit improvements. Changes to margin also could be muted by underutilization of production and any inflation in input prices. Management comments about other operating expenses ("opex") will also be needed regarding future cash flow generation.

Capex guidance is another sign. If management states it will stick to the full $14B FY2025 plans, that will positively impact sentiment. I don’t expect guidance to be softened, especially as we’re gaining more clarity on the macroeconomy following a period of international trade disruption.

Forward-looking comments about the company’s end markets will help determine how strong and broad the current recovery is. AI/data center demand seems strong, but there will be a better indication that the recovery is sustainable if the mobile and PC end markets are showing signs of particular strength. Comments about inventory will also aid understanding of supply-demand equilibrium across customers and internally.

If management is still speaking confidently about corporate momentum and demand, that could support a longer holding period. At the moment, my exit strategy is shorter-term for this particular stock. If management is vague or changes tone and discusses 2026 with caution, that would add to the concern level about the cycle stabilizing or maturing. It could also rationally prompt a quicker exit for shrewd investors looking for capital security.

Conclusion: Buy

Micron is still a compelling buy, particularly in light of the firm's leadership in AI (memory) and pending expansion plans. But a defense strategy against cyclical dynamics is also called for. Micron’s current valuation embeds optimism of a stable longer-term growth phase. Yields and returns in the memory segment are improving as lower-cost, more efficient memory experiences a structural demand boost driven by AI and data centers. However, this trend also brings some risk to Micron’s capital expenditures efficacy—and the broader memory cycle and geopolitical headwinds remain primary concerns. In my view, being nimble and catching Micron’s current strong return prospects is shrewd, but it will be important to be tactical with your price objective.

data.title: HCI Vs American Coastal Insurance: HCI Continues To Be Undervalued Despite A Solid Rally
data.description:
Investment Thesis

HCI Group (NYSE:HCI) and American Coastal Insurance Corporation (NASDAQ:ACIC) are the property and casualty insurance (P&C insurance) issuers mostly concentrated in the Florida region and neighboring coastal areas. HCI is the bigger company with the product mix that caters to homeowners, landlords, and the commercial sector, while ACIC has recently sold its subsidiary to solely service the commercial sector.

Year-to-date, HCI's stock has rallied by 36%, while ACIC's has declined by 20%. Such divergence in performance tends to create a spread trade opportunity for long-short funds to bet on the spread closing. Assessing each stock's fundamental metrics, I don't think this is a good setup for such a trade, and even though both stocks still continue to be undervalued relative to the sector, investors are safe betting on the outperformer.

Historical average returns

When we look at the historical average returns, HCI has performed well relative to ACIC, SPY (SPY), and the Property and Casualty Insurance ETF (KBWP). Returning 17% in 10 years while SPY and KBWP returned 13% and ACIC returned less than 1%. Given that the P&C insurance market increased by 7.7% last year and with more growth expected as we continue to see catastrophic events worsen and the world becoming more uncertain, HCI still has more room to grow both in revenue and market cap.

ACIC's low performance can mostly be explained by its disappointing quarterly revenue growth in the past 5 years. From June 30, 2020, to the recent quarter, ACIC's revenue declined by 66%, while HCI's revenue increased by 167%.

Net loss ratio

We are all familiar with this concept: with great returns comes great exposure to risk. When we look at the revenue charts above, we can see that HCI appears to be more volatile compared to ACIC. This is because HCI is aggressively growing market share while ACIC plays a conservative card to keep the lights on. This is also explained by their net loss ratio below. ACIC puts more effort into maintaining a net loss ratio of below 30%, while HCI has a higher and more volatile chart. HCI's management has stated its commitment to reducing its loss ratio in a recent earnings call. During the first three (03) quarters of 2023, the net loss ratio averaged around 55%, and coming into 2024, the chart trended nicely to below 45%. But we started to see increasing claims, which spiked the chart to more than 60% in the second half of 2024 due to the three hurricanes (Debby, Helene, and Milton) that affected the Florida citizens and increased the number of claims from policyholders. Looking forward, I'm more optimistic that HCI will normalize the net loss ratio to below 55% as the company starts to de-risk its portfolio.

Financial Results

HCI Income Statement

In Q1 of 2025, HCI increased its gross premiums by 17% compared to Q1 of 2024 and ceded $99.63 million, retaining $200.75 million in net premiums. That was a 46% and 6% increase, respectively. Total revenue increased by 5% to $216.43 million, beating revenue estimates by $1.54 million. Operating expenses declined by 11%; this was caused by a 26% decline in incurred losses & loss adjustment expenses, showing management's commitment to decreasing the net loss ratio by ceding more of its premium. As of the last quarter Ceded premium now makes up about 33% of its gross premium. The 11% decline in OPEX increased pretax income and net income by 30%, bringing EPS to $5.35, a $0.47 and $0.79 beat of GAAP and normalized EPS estimates.

ACIC Income Statement

Looking at ACIC's Q1 income statement, total revenue increased by 8% yoy to $72.19 million, missing revenue estimates by $7.84 million. This revenue growth wasn't organic, as it was due to the company lowering its ceded premiums by 4% while growing gross premiums by only 1%. Incurred losses & loss adjustment expenses decreased by 9%, and G&A decreased by 15%, while the policy and underwriting costs increased by more than double. This resulted in a 33% increase in operating expenses and a 16% decline in operating income, which eventually led to net income declining by 10%, beating EPS estimates by only $0.02. The drastic increase in policy & underwriting costs was caused by high acquisition costs, which were due to fewer ceding commissions from reinsurers and high management fees. I understand why the company is lowering ceded premiums; as of the last quarter, the ceded premiums made up about 58% of ACIC's gross premiums. Even though this lessens the company's risk surface, it affects the bottom-line performance because more money passes through to reinsurers.

HCI Balance sheet

HCI's balance sheet shows tremendous strength and liquidity. Total investments and cash grew by 31% and 15%, respectively. Most of these investments are government bonds, and about $510 million are due for sale in a year or less, as disclosed in the 10Q. With the cash at hand, that is enough liquidity to cover the $798 million in policy claim liabilities, which increased by 38%. Reinsurance recoverables increased by 60% to $527.8 million, which implies that HCI is starting to pursue a risk-off strategy by ceding an increasing portion of its gross premiums. Looking at the liabilities side, long-term debt remained flat over the year, while unearned premiums increased by 15%, showing growth in new policy underwriting. Overall, total assets increased by 25%, liabilities by 22%, and shareholder's equity increased by 37%. On the earnings call, management further guided for another 37% increase in shareholder's equity in Q2 to close to three-quarters of $1 billion ($750 million). This guidance comes as the company expects to redeem 4.75% of its convertible senior notes, which is estimated to reduce the debt on its balance sheet by $172 million.

ACIC Balance sheet

ACIC also has a healthy balance sheet, with total investments growing by 69% to the value of $335.8 million. Cash declined by 41%, which could be explained by the surge in claim payments made in the third and the fourth quarters of 2024. Reinsurance recoverables also decreased by 21%, and ceded unearned premiums decreased by 12%. This shows that while HCI is de-risking more of its portfolio to reinsurers to lower its net loss ratio, ACIC is doing the opposite to improve its underwriting margins. The problem is unearned premium was flat over the year, showing top-line stagnation, as this implies the company is not underwriting new policies as frequently. Given this reality, margins could remain compressed even if fewer premiums get ceded. Overall, total assets increased by 8%, total liabilities increased by 3%, and shareholder's equity increased by 28%.

Quant ratings

Looking at the Seeking Alpha quant rating now vs. a year ago for HCI, we can see that the quant system made a great call rating HCI a strong buy while it was trading at $96 per share. Since then the stock has increased by 63%. Now, looking currently, not much has changed: HCI is still rated A+ for momentum, A- for profitability, growth upgraded to an A, valuation slightly downgraded to a B, and revisions drastically downgraded to a C. This has caused an overall downgrade of HCI from a strong buy to a buy. I also expect revenue and EPS to not grow significantly for HCI going forward, as it intends to reduce its net loss ratio, which might affect its underwriting margins a little bit.

The quant system rated ACIC a hold a year ago—great call again, as the stock price has remained flat since then. The system has downgraded ACIC to a sell with growth, profitability, momentum, and EPS revisions downgraded to a D-, B, D-, and D, respectively. However, the valuation has been upgraded from an A- to an A. Personally, I don't see any further decline in the stock price, and at this attractive valuation, selling might not be as smart.

Valuation

Both HCI and ACIC remain to be undervalued relative to their sector, with HCI trading at a 10% discount and ACIC trading at a 33% discount on a forward P/E ratio. Even with a 36% stock performance YTD, there's still room for HCI to continue outperforming, while ACIC signals to be a classical value trap that is nearing the bottom. Given the solid fundamentals HCI has, I don't see any slowdown in stock performance and believe the spread between HCI and ACIC will continue to widen.

Risks

As we enter the second half of 2025, hurricanes and other catastrophic events are likely to happen, which will obviously be a short-term headwind for both stocks, causing more claims and compressing margin returns. HCI continues to have a higher net loss ratio than ACIC, meaning it is much more vulnerable to these catastrophes. It should be stated, though, that the company managed to grow shareholder equity by $150 million last year despite having to deal with three (03) hurricanes. However, past performance is not indicative of future performance. Investors should also take note of the news that HCI was listed as one of the companies that are going to leave the Russell microcap index, even though I do not consider this to be a massive risk, but the index outflows could potentially result in high volatility in HCI's stock.

Conclusion

In closing, I find HCI to be well positioned for an upside revaluation as the market continues to appreciate the quality metrics of this company, i.e., double-digit revenue growth, decreasing operating expenses, and a highly liquid, de-levered balance sheet. Meanwhile, ACIC has stagnant top-line growth, compressed profit margins, and a historically bad performance. Not everything is bearish on ACIC; the company has a healthy balance sheet, and it has a less risky portfolio. With that said, I rate HCI a buy and ACIC a hold.

Kindly note that going forward I will start to do more comparative analyses. This came from feedback I received from my prior articles; one was (GAMB) vs. (RSI) and the other was (AL) vs. (AER). These articles got positive feedback from my followers and SA editors.

Fortunately, these articles happened to be one of my favorite write-ups and the type of content I find to be lacking in most research platforms. So going forward, expect more comparative analysis from me; however, I will continue to do single stock write-ups occasionally. Your feedback is highly appreciated.

data.title: Shopify Can Easily Cross $150B+ Market Cap In H2 2025
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Shopify (SHOP) in March, I rated the stock a “buy” with a price target of $125. Although the stock dropped soon afterwards given the market volatility from post-Liberation Day announcements, it has been able to climb back up, with the stock now down just 1.5% since the time of my previous publication.

Shopify reported their Q1 FY25 earnings in May, where revenue growth slowed sequentially to 27% YoY as expected, with an operating margin of 9%, which also shrank on a quarter-over-quarter basis.

While the slowdown was already priced in, their total GMV (Gross Merchandise Volume) grew 23%, marking the seventh consecutive quarter of twenty percent-plus growth rates, as the company continued to see strength in its International, Offline and Enterprise/B2B markets. Moreover, the penetration of Shopify Payments as a percentage of GMV continued to deepen, especially as the company launched it in 16 new countries during the quarter.

Although the current macroeconomic environment remains challenging, the management remained confident in their ability to navigate the landscape given the quality and diversity of their merchant and user base, as well as the agility of their “merchant-first” product innovation.

Looking forward, I continue to remain bullish on Shopify, as I believe that there is plenty of whitespace that the company can penetrate, especially as it expands across the types of merchants across geographies by innovating on their product portfolio, thus solidifying their value proposition as the “unified operating system for commerce”.

As a result, I will reiterate my price target of $124, which implies an 18% upside or a market cap that should reach close to $160B by the end of the year.

Deepening Penetration Of Shopify Payments And Merchant-First Product Innovation

Shopify reported their Q1 FY25 earnings last month, where revenue grew 27% YoY to $2.36B, while GMV grew close to 23% YoY, marking the seventh consecutive quarter of twenty percent-plus growth rates, driven by growth in both same-store sales and new merchant acquisitions.

Out of the $2.36B in revenue, Merchant Solutions revenue grew 29% to $1.74B, with the penetration of Shopify Payments reaching 65% of GMV, up from 60% last year, while Subscription Solutions revenue increased 21% YoY to $620M as a result of new merchant additions, variable platform fees, and a growing adoption of Shopify Plus subscription plan.

With a value proposition that is centered on building a unified operating system for commerce, Shopify has always taken a “merchant-first” focus when it comes to product innovation.

During the quarter, the company continued to expand the breadth of their product portfolio by enhancing their managed markets products for cross-border trade, thus enabling their US merchants to achieve compliance with ease. At the same time, Shopify also introduced a feature on their Shop App that allows buyers to filter products by country, thus promoting local businesses.

Plus, in order to help merchants lower their duty rates when it comes to sourcing products from different countries in the current macroeconomic climate, Shopify launched their AI-driven tariff tool called tariffguide.ai while simultaneously simplifying international shipping by expanding their network of third-party logistics providers through the Shopify Fulfillment Network App.

This has helped the company drive deeper adoption of its platform, with continuing strength across all their revenue cohorts, as can be seen below. In fact, according to management commentary, 38 out of 39 of their quarterly merchant cohorts have outperformed the overall e-commerce market, indicating the platform’s ability to drive growth and resilience.

3 Growth Levers To Capture TAM: International, Offline And Enterprise

Despite Shopify growing robustly ever since it IPO’ed in 2015, their core market still has plenty of runway, as they continue to add more products, expand across more geographies, and serve merchants of varying sizes to capture market share in a growing TAM (Total Addressable Market).

In fact, per the slide below, Shopify has only penetrated just 2% of opportunities in geographies they currently serve, leaving plenty of whitespace to expand into in the coming years.

Particularly, when it comes to its growth levers, I want to briefly discuss three of its strategic priorities.

Let’s start with Shopify’s International markets, which saw GMV grow 31% YoY, compared to 33% in the previous quarter, driven by growth across both small merchants and enterprises in key European markets that include the UK, Netherlands, and Germany.

During the quarter, the company launched Shopify Payments in 16 new markets, bringing the total to 39 countries that are now supported by Shopify Payments, thus offering merchants a streamlined payment solution with lower fees and enhanced security, as well as access to features like Shop Pay that provide greater convenience for buyers. Plus, they also launched multicurrency payouts in 20 countries across Europe, thus helping buyers minimize the impact of fluctuating exchange rates.

Meanwhile, Shopify’s Offline GMV also grew 23% YoY as a result of strength in both their mid-market and multi-location merchants.

Finally, the company continues to make progress with moving upmarket. During the quarter, Shopify reported that its B2B GMV delivered triple-digit growth of 109% YoY. One of the products that the management credited for its success in moving upmarket is Shop Pay, where it acts as a strategic entry point for enterprise brands to enter the Shopify ecosystem, followed by a gradual adoption across other products.

Plus, in the current macroeconomic environment, where legacy platforms are struggling to adapt rapidly, Shopify is able to help merchants quickly navigate the evolving landscape, particularly around tariffs, with ease and efficiency, thus enabling them to onboard larger companies across geographies.

Gross Margins Shrank YoY, But Operating Expenses As A Percentage Of Revenue Trended Lower

One of the things that the management reiterated numerous times on the earnings call was their commitment to their operating discipline, which they believe is key to providing the flexibility they need to ensure long-term growth.

During the quarter, Shopify saw its gross margins expand on a sequential basis by 140 basis points to 49.5%. However, on a year-over-year basis, gross margins declined close to 190 basis points as a result of higher cloud and infrastructure hosting costs needed to support higher volumes and geographic expansion.

In the meantime, the company generated $203M in operating income, which grew over 136% YoY with a margin expansion of 400 basis points to 9%, with operating expenses that continue to trend lower as a percentage of total revenue (as can be seen below), as the management remains committed to growing operating leverage by building a lean, flexible, and highly efficient team.

Tariff Risks Remain, Although Management Is Confident It Can Navigate Through With Ease

Although Shopify is expected to grow in the twenties range over the coming years as per consensus estimates, the current macroeconomic uncertainty around tariffs and its impact on the consumer and business demand landscape can induce volatility in the company.

So far, the management has reiterated that they haven’t seen any “meaningful” impact on GMV, given the quality and diversity of their merchant base and buyers, which the management believes provides “unique stability” to their business and allows them to navigate changing macroeconomic conditions with ease.

At the same time, the management also noted that they haven’t seen broad-based price increases across goods sold by merchants on their platform amid tariff pressures. Even if merchants were to raise prices in the future, the management believes that they are more insulated from demand shocks as their buyer base skews towards higher-income consumers, who tend to be less price sensitive.

So far, the company’s track record on delivering earnings surprises has been quite uneven, while it has consistently beat revenue guidance. Looking forward, the tariff environment is one to watch out for and how it impacts GMV growth across all its markets. The way I see it, as long as GMV growth stays in the 20%+ range, with growing penetration of Shopify Payments, investor sentiment should remain anchored.

Revisiting My Valuation

As Shopify continues to expand the types of merchants they serve across geographies by helping them grow their businesses with the help of innovative solutions that deepen their usage across the platform, I believe that the company has tremendous runway left in its $849B TAM.

From a revenue perspective, Shopify is expected to grow in the twenties range over the coming years, which, I believe, is quite achievable, as it doubles down on its growth levers of International, Offline and Enterprise/B2B markets with growing penetration of Shopify Payments and Subscription Solutions. This will translate to an estimated revenue of close to $16B by FY27.

When it comes to profitability, assuming that Shopify can reach gross margins of 52% (up from 50% in FY24) by FY27, along with streamlining its operating expenses to 35% of total revenue during this period of time, we arrive at a total operating income of $2.7B, with a margin of 17%, up from 12% in FY24.

This means that the company is expected to grow its operating income at a compounded annual growth rate of 36% over the coming three years, as opposed to the average company in the S&P 500 that grows its earnings at a pace of 8%. Therefore, assigning it a price-to-earnings multiple of 77, which is roughly 4.5 times that of the average S&P 500 multiple, we arrive at a price target of $124, which represents an upside of 18% from its current levels, or a market cap of close to $160B.

My Final Verdict And Conclusions

Although the management remains confident in their ability to navigate the current macroeconomic landscape around tariffs, we need to monitor the trend of GMV in the short term to assess the shift in the demand landscape.

However, over the mid- to long-term, I believe the stock is headed significantly higher, with the market cap of Shopify likely exceeding $160B by the end of the year as it continues to grab market share by expanding across the types of merchants they serve across geographies and building targeted solutions that further deepen adoption of the platform.

data.title: Discount Dividends: 2 Irresistible Bargains Hiding In Plain Sight
data.description:
Introduction

I have good news.

The market is close to its all-time high again, erasing a decline of roughly 20%, which made it one of five corrections over the past ten years.

While it needs to be seen just how bad news the Iran war headlines are for the market, there is no denying that the world's biggest stock index is holding up impressively well.

This has a number of reasons, including the assurance that we are, in fact, not headed for a global trade war that could blow up supply chains. While we are still in a headline-driven market with tariff risks, the worst-case scenario has not happened, causing investors to slowly but steadily bet on a "return to normal."

It also helps that the Federal Reserve is increasingly expected to lower rates, as Fed funds futures (which always need to be taken with a grain of salt) indicate four rate cuts over the next 12 months, potentially lowering rates to the range of 3.25% to 3.50%, all fueled by the absence of any upside momentum in inflation numbers, even after tariffs have been implemented.

While all of this is good news, we are not out of the woods yet, as Bloomberg's John Authers wrote in an opinion piece titled "Waiting for the Fed All Summer Long" earlier this week.

He brought up one of the most important points I can think of, which is the hidden risk of higher-for-longer inflation.

One problem is that the underlying numbers simply don't look that good yet. It's one of the reasons why I believe in a "higher for longer" inflation environment, as the underlying inflation beast hasn't been defeated yet.

Over the last three months, flexible prices dipped, possibly due to weak demand, while sticky price inflation declined very slowly. The Cleveland Fed’s measure of the trimmed mean, which excludes outliers and averages the rest, ticked upward for the first time this year, and remains above 3% — another sign that underlying inflation pressures remain. - Bloomberg

These problems include the "supercore" inflation number, which is services excluding shelter. This index is heavily watched by the Federal Reserve, as inflation in services has been a major problem for the economy since the pandemic.
