That's because inflation in goods worked its way through the economy, hitting the one area that hurts the most, which is services.

Also, to make matters worse, goods inflation is making a comeback.

These developments make sense, as manufacturing companies noted a steep increase in delivery times and prices paid, two leading indicators for inflation, as it usually takes a few months for these prices to work their way through the supply chain.

Also, I am worried about oil.

By that, I mean the impact of oil on inflation. Due to OPEC aggressively boosting output and economic growth fears, oil has made it to the low-$60s. Without that benefit to inflation, these numbers would look very different.

That's one of the reasons why I expect any meaningful increase in oil to further put a strong foundation below inflation and make it harder for the Fed to cut rates.

The chart below shows what the price of oil (black line) compared to the all-item year-over-year inflation rate in the United States (purple line) looks like:

That's why I'm going back to John Authers again, as he brought up comments from ABN Amro Investment Solutions, which summarize perfectly the challenges that some may be missing:

  • Slow disinflation is not enough to prompt the Fed to change its stance. Only a major downside surprise on jobs data, which seems unlikely, may urge it to cut rates.
  • If there is a Liberation Day Part 2 where Trump announces widespread tariffs, including a 50% tariff on Europe, the risk of a Trumpcession may resurface.

To make matters worse, this challenging market environment comes with a lofty valuation. Because of the market's rebound, the valuation has become anything but cheap.

As much fun as I had buying bargains in April, the fact that the S&P 500's earnings yield premium has dropped to a near two-decade low makes it much harder to find great stocks at attractive prices.

The data above is extremely important, as the equity risk premium compares the S&P 500's earnings yield to the yield investors get for buying the 10-year bond.

Essentially, the market's earnings yield is the inverse P/E ratio. In order to explain why this matters, imagine that this is the dividend yield of the market if all earnings were to be paid through dividends. The higher, the more value investors are buying.

When comparing the number to the yield on risk-free bonds, we get the relative attractiveness of stocks.

Objectively and generally speaking, right now, stocks are not that attractive, as the earnings yield is less than the yield on 10-year bonds.

That's partially why I expect the total return of the S&P 500 to be subdued over the next 5–10 years, at least compared to the past ten years.

However, I am not shying away from stocks. On the contrary. As most readers will know, I dislike bonds because I prefer to use my hard-earned cash to buy stakes in fantastic companies instead of lending it to governments.

That's why I am spending so much time on finding stocks that come with attractive valuations, fantastic business models, dividend growth/income, and a high likelihood to generate substantial alpha when it matters most.

So, in light of everything we discussed, which includes sticky inflation, growth challenges, and valuation headwinds, I decided to focus on two stocks that deliver exactly what I am looking for.

However, to justify this very bullish title, I made sure to really emphasize the valuation part, as I expect both stocks to be realistically undervalued by 50-100% over the next 3–5 years.

Permian Resources Corp. (PR) - One Of The Best Risk-Reward Ratios On The Market

I was 100% sure I was going to include an energy stock in this article.

Historically speaking, energy stocks are the best place to be in times of elevated inflation, as they have the highest percentage of positive total returns and the highest outperformance when inflation averages 3% or more on a longer-term basis.

This means energy stocks are perfect for my thesis.

It also means I could mention at least a dozen great energy picks.

However, for this specific article, I decided to go with Permian Resources, a company I started covering in 2024, when I called it "The Cheapest Oil Stock You've Never Heard Of."

Permian Resources is NOT for the faint of heart, as its market cap of $10.4 billion makes it much smaller than the reliable dividend energy companies like Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX) you may be more familiar with. It's also much more volatile. So, please keep that in mind when doing your own due diligence.

With that said, I also believe that PR is much more promising than most of its bigger peers. For a wide range of reasons, actually.

One reason is its focus on the Permian Basin, America's biggest oil basin. It's also the only major basin capable of consistent output growth, thanks to large untapped resources in both the Midland and Delaware sub-basins.

Headquartered in Midland, Texas, Permian Resources' assets cover roughly 450,000 net acres and 88,000 net royalty acres, which are mainly located in Eddy and Lea Counties, New Mexico, and Reeves and Ward Counties, Texas. These are some of my favorite places to be and major areas of operations of LandBridge (LB) and Texas Pacific Land (TPL), my two biggest investments.

The company, which went through the 2015/2016 energy crash as Colgate Energy, has a strategy built on three pillars, which are cost leadership, a strong balance sheet, and opportunistic investments during market downturns.

While the company is highly dependent on energy prices, which limits its ability to generate value beyond traditional energy operations, it has found an edge thanks to characteristics like a high oil share of total revenues. As of 1Q25, oil accounted for 47% of its revenues, with high-margin liquids boosting that number to 70%.

It also had a net leverage ratio of just 0.8x, one of the best balance sheets in the entire industry, which also comes with a robust hedging position, as PR has hedged a quarter of its production with a floor of $73 WTI.

While I prefer unhedged companies during oil bull markets, it's a great strategy that actually beats even peers like Diamondback (FANG) when it comes to downside protection. I'm impressed by that.

Moreover, it has done tremendously well when it comes to operating efficiencies, growing its oil production by 2% since 4Q24 with 4% lower cash costs per barrel and a 3% decline in drilling and completion costs.

On top of that, it has more than 15 years of high-quality inventory, which is consistently expanding through bolt-on acquisitions and new discoveries.

The best thing, however, is that Permian Resources is really good at distributing cash to shareholders. Instead of simply buying back stock and paying a dividend, the company is great at timing.

Currently, it has a buyback authorization of $1 billion (nearly 10% of its market cap). In April, it bought back $43 million of stock. While that's not a lot, it did so at the bottom of its stock price, with goals to use future dips to deploy more capital. This makes future corrections great opportunities to improve the risk/reward of the company.

It also declared a quarterly base cash dividend of $0.15 per share, which translates to $0.60 per year. At current prices, that's a 4.0% yield that's fully breakeven at $40 WTI, a number that many of its peers cannot match.

That's why I expect aggressive, double-digit, annual dividend growth if I'm right and oil starts a long-term bull market in the quarters ahead.

It also remains one of the cheapest stocks.

Compared to its peers, it has one of the lowest enterprise value multiples. Although the stock price has risen by roughly 17% since the overview below was made, it shows the company trades at a substantial discount to larger peers.

While it deserves to trade at a small discount due to its smaller size and the fact that investors still feel that the company needs to prove itself, its balance sheet and operational excellence warrant a much smaller gap to peers like Diamondback.

In fact, PR trades at just 3x operating cash flow, a number I consider to be ridiculously cheap, as it's way below its long-term average of 5.2x, and even further below the 7-9x average I consider to be appropriate for high-quality Permian drillers.

As a result, I expect PR to double over the next 12–24 months. That's my base case. A stronger oil price uptrend could unlock a lot more value.

Canadian National Railway (CNI) - A Canadian Gem That's Perfect For What's Next

I like low-risk contrarian investments. Investments that usually perform well but are out of favor due to issues like cyclical growth headwinds.

That's why I have taken a shine to cyclical stocks this year. While I fully acknowledge the economic headwinds facing the market in almost every single article I wrote, it's one of my favorite places to put money in a market that has become quite expensive again.

In May, for example, I wrote an article titled "Buried Under Fear: One Of The Best Opportunities I've Seen In Years." One of the companies I should have included in that article is the Canadian National Railway, a company whose roots go back to 1919, when it was created out of seven bankrupt railroads that fell into the hands of the Canadian government.

Back then, railroading was hard, as competition was fierce. There were simply too many railroads on the market. There was not a lot of money to be made unless you had the best assets and a unique game plan.

Now, that's different, as North America is home to just six major Class I railroads, with one of these being Buffett's private BNSF, which can only be owned through Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A).

In fact, all Class I railroads have wide-moat business models thanks to very limited competition, dominant positions in the areas they serve, strong pricing power, efficiency benefits compared to on-the-road trucking, and other things like healthy balance sheets.

Canadian National is one of them, as it operates a huge rail network of roughly 20,000 route miles across Canada and the United States, which connects three coasts: the Atlantic, the Pacific, and the Gulf of Mexico.

Moreover, the company transports a wide and diverse range of goods, including bulk commodities (grain, coal, potash), intermodal, forest products, and energy products like petroleum and chemicals. This makes it "mission-critical" in almost every supply chain you can think of.

In general, I like that just roughly a fifth of its revenue comes from intermodal, with most revenues being generated by higher-margin bulk and merchandise goods. Intermodal is more dependent on consumer sentiment and has stronger competition from trucks carrying containers from ports to customers. It's not a bad business, but I simply prefer bulk and merch.

This has allowed the company's New York-listed shares to return 3,520% since January 1, 2000, beating the 540% return of the market by a wide margin.

Also, note that industrial stocks have beaten the market since then, which, I believe, is due to the wide-moat business models of many industrial stocks. While most don't have the high-tech advantages of some of today's high-flying companies, they tend to come with fewer disruption risks and more consistent growth. That's one of the reasons why I like industrial stocks too much.

Anyway, like all of its peers, since 2021, CNI has been a disappointment. That's due to a peak in cyclical growth expectations, worsened by trade war fears, labor disruption, and smaller factors.

However, the risk/reward is getting good!

On top of achieving 8% EPS growth in 1Q25, the company has seen velocity metrics reaching levels not seen in May for several years, as the Canadian is doing what it does best, which is boosting its operating efficiencies.

It also reiterated its full-year guidance last month, expecting 10% to 15% EPS growth despite tough volume comparisons in the second quarter. Even better, the company expects a stronger performance in the second half of the year, driven by easier comparisons, company-specific growth initiatives, and a potential economic uptick, which is why I wrote my "Buried In Fear" article.

These company-specific investments include customer-driven volume growth from fracking sand supply for LNG Canada, liquid exports to Japan, and new crush facilities in Saskatchewan and Ohio.

On a long-term basis, the company targets high-single-digit adjusted EPS growth, growing faster than the economy, with pricing ahead of rail inflation.

This also means that if we were to get a cyclical growth upswing, the company could see substantially higher growth rates.

Analysts agree with that, as they expect 16%, 11%, and 10% EPS growth in 2025, 2026, and 2027, respectively, potentially marking the first sustainable upswing since the pandemic.

As CNI trades at just 19.7x earnings, below its 10-year average of 21.3x, there's a path to a fair price target of $150 in New York, roughly 50% above its current price.

That's why I absolutely love the risk/reward.

If I did not already own Union Pacific (UNP) and Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP), CNI would be a holding of mine as well, as there are not a lot of people who love railroads more than me.

The cherry on top of its dividend.

Currently yielding 2.5%, the dividend has been raised every single year since 1995, with a 15% CAGR since 1996. This year, the hike was 5%, which I expect to be boosted in the years ahead. It has a sub-50% payout ratio and an A-range credit rating, providing additional safety.

All things considered, just like PR, CNI is a Strong Buy in my book.

Takeaway

The market's resilience is impressive. However, we are not out of the woods yet.

Inflation remains sticky, oil is a wildcard, and valuations have become stretched again. However, I'm not sitting on the sidelines. As always, I'm hunting for stocks that offer exceptional value in this environment.

Permian Resources is one of my high-conviction energy plays, as it's a lean, efficient operator with a rock-solid balance sheet, aggressive buybacks, and a dividend that could soar if oil rallies.

Meanwhile, Canadian National Railway is a cyclical gem trading at a steep discount, with earnings poised to accelerate and a dividend that's grown nonstop since my birth year of 1995.

All things considered, both stocks have realistic 50-100% upside potential in the next few years. Depending on one's growth profile, they could make great additions to a wide variety of dividend portfolios.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk is prolonged cyclical weakness, which could pressure oil demand, transportation volumes, and general market sentiment. Specific risks are more OPEC output hikes, tariffs on Canada that could disrupt cross-border flows, and operational struggles in CNI's network.

data.title: Ategrity Specialty Holdings: A Strong Specialty Insurance Offering
data.description:
Shares of Ategrity Specialty Holdings (NYSE:ASIC) have seen a rather strong public debut. Shares of the specialty insurance firm have come strong out of the gate, and have risen as investors like the focused operations, earnings and reasonable valuations at the offer price.

While these qualities are demonstrated in the growth of the business, I am not a big investor in insurance operations, given the inherent risks involved, although I recognize that the industry at large has a solid track record in creating value for investors.

A Specialty Insurance Firm

Ategrity Specialty describes itself as a growing specialty insurance business which focuses on so-called "excess and surplus" products, which it offers to small and medium-sized businesses. The specific segment focuses on insurance of items which standard carriers are not covering.

Special verticals on which the firm focuses include retail, real estate, hospitality and construction. The company focuses mostly on short-term and medium-term policies, with premiums for nearly two-thirds tied towards casualty, and the remainder from property insurance.

The company has developed a proprietary underwriting platform which combines sophisticated data analytics with automated and streamlined processes to serve clients and shareholders. To serve end clients, the company uses over 500 distribution partners as customers have greater need for such tech-enabled solutions.

The company generates revenues in 48 states, yet the combined revenues generated from California, Florida and Texas totals about 50% of revenues. With some of these states vulnerable to specific (natural) disasters, that is suboptimal.

Valuation and IPO Thoughts

Ategrity Specialty aimed to sell 6.7 million shares in a preliminary price range between $14 and $16 per share, with final pricing set at $17 per share. With all shares sold by the business itself, gross proceeds from the offering came in at $114 million, that is, of course, excluding the over-allotment option.

With just over 47 million shares outstanding, the equity of the business is valued at $799 million at the offer price. The balance sheet is a bit hard to read, given the nature of the business and its operations.

The same complexity is observed in the profit and loss statement. The company reported net premiums earned of $231 million in 2023, with investment income driving total revenues to $241 million. The company reported just $12 million in operating income, with the loss ratio reported around two-thirds, and the expense ratio topping 30% of sales.

Net premiums earned rose to over $290 million in 2024, with total revenues up to $343 million and change, driven by much higher investment income and alike. This resulted in operating expenses having risen to a $66 million number, with net earnings reported at $47 million, which incidentally was equal to a dollar per share.

Net premium earned for the first quarter of 2025 rose by more than ten million to $78 million, as the increase in total revenues was a bit less due to a lower net sum of investment income and gains on investments. Amidst higher costs, operating earnings were down by an increment, with GAAP earnings reported at $8 million and change.

While revenues are growing, I conservatively look for earnings to come in flat this year, suggesting that earnings come in around a dollar per share, as the company might see some benefits from the gross proceeds of the offering. This could boost after-tax income by a few million and thereby a couple of pennies per share as well.

As shares have risen to $23 in the days following the offering, the market value of the firm tops a billion, and with earnings power seen around a dollar per share, or just above that, multiples are rather demanding here.

Concluding Thoughts

I am not too keen on investing in insurance businesses, despite the float available to invest. As an industry outsider, it is hard to judge the risk strategic of such firms and investments, as I fear adverse outcomes for insurers.

Risk factors have much to do with underwriting and losses in adverse events, fierce competition, an inability to obtain reinsurance, counterparty credit risk, reliance on distribution partners, extreme weather, concentrated regions in which policies are underwritten, and the fact that the company is a controlled company, with shareholder ZFSG holding a controlling stake in the business.

All this, but mostly plain valuations, make it very easy to avoid the shares after the public offering. This follows solid gains in the first days of trading, pushing up multiples to a premium over the market at large here. This makes me very cautious here, as I am taking a wait-and-see-approach to learn more about the business and its performance in the coming quarters, but in no rush to get involved here.

data.title: Clarus: Promotionality Hitting Margins
data.description:
Clarus (CLAR) did not manage to drive growth yet as it continues to simplify its business aiming to reach a steady state profitability. Being in transition, they are hit by a few non-recurring mix effects and also some other transitory costs. They have withdrawn guidance on uncertainty around recent May pricing actions and also questions around margin effects from tariffs which have required considerable price increases on their Black Diamond flagship branded products. While the transition will likely help underlying economics, we don't really see the business becoming a profitable grower very soon, particularly as they attempt price increases to offset tariffs. As of now, considering also the possible dangers on the demand side, multiples are looking quite in line with peers as well, though it's difficult to say with the business operating around breakeven, and P/S multiples being less useful as we wonder about terminal profitability. We wait and see.

Latest Quarter

As opposed to the FY 2024, the latest quarter saw a weaker result, particularly in international adventure. Revenue declines were occurring here due to quite a lot of promotionality on slow-moving inventory, pushed through an off-price retailer. This also had a negative mix effect. This shouldn't be recurring, but it's part of the voiding of inventory that they are likely to deprioritise going forward. Tickets weren't that high on account of this, and there were also general delays to major retailers in key markets. It appears that these OEM delays should be mostly a negative timing effect, and mostly explain the declines in Q1, but will also accompany difficulties in returning demand from the existing distributors to normalised levels.

Outdoor is also experiencing negative mix effects. The share of discontinued products in the mix is up by around 50%, as they exit businesses like bindings and also leave snow safety. It is being investigated by the DoJ over the avalanche safety, being recently served subpoenas. This is generating legal fees that explains more than 20% of the net segment operating loss this quarter. The business is getting simplified at least, but the pushing of discontinued merchandise has resulted in significant promotionality in this segment as well, and the discontinuing of the businesses also means significant sales impacts, with the ski category down 38% in North America wholesale. Also, the business is getting hit by some timing effects where a better cadence was established that frontloaded some of the sales to distributors into the Q4 at the expense of the current Q1, to our understanding. But things are getting a little more efficient. D2C unit economics have started to improve in outdoor NA sales.

Bottom Line

There are some bright spots. While sales are still down, bookings are looking better, with a good order book for the fall/winter season with apparel bookings at least up 30-50% depending on geography compared to last year. Black Diamond is now also set up with ecommerce. Headcount is 25% lower, sales are down by less, so there has been at least a per-head productivity improvement. Gross margins are also especially bad this quarter due to the aggressive promotionality to clear unwanted inventory.

On the other hand, the steel and aluminium tariffs have required price increases, just instituted this May, and while management reports things look alright as of the earnings call, there is a risk that markets don't absorb this pricing action favourably. Then there are the general macro questions around the impact of tariffs. At least on the cost side the exposure could be worse. It seems that 25% of the total merchandise cost for the company is from China. It is focused on adventure which is mostly China exposed but also more international facing. Therefore, the exposure shouldn't be too bad to China at least where higher tariffs are likely to stick. Brands like Black Diamond have 25% overall exposure on the supply side to China. But they are accelerating plans to move away. Still, there are the other universal and steel and aluminium tariffs that will have an important effect, and they are trying to offset these tariffs with price increases that are quite considerable.

Then there is also this emerging legal matter, that we are not qualified to comment on in detail other than to say it adds to the uncertainty.

While outdoor EBIT margins have improved on simplification despite negative mix effects, it may struggle to see enough improvement to help cover the corporate costs. Corporate costs on an adjusted basis were 2.3 million USD. They are still a ways away from solid profitability. We are going to wait and see at this point. We want to know how the sales materialise towards the end of the year in the current consumer environment, particularly as they attempt to pass higher steel and aluminum, or Chinese tariff costs, onto consumers. We are not confident that pricing action will be well met by the current consumer environment in general, as a macro question in the wake of tariffs. We also want to see how the attenuation of these transitory negative mix effects resolve into results, and also how the new delivery cadence looks over the full year.
